Reception history may focus on the reception of a specific philosopher, such as Baruch Spinoza, a particular work, such as his _Ethics_ (1677), or a specific idea, such as pantheism or the identity of God and nature. Historians of philosophy have always discussed reception. Just think of scholarship on Aristotle’s engagement with Plato’s philosophy or scholarship on medieval philosophy where translations of Aristotle’s works and commentaries on these works constitute an essential part of the source materials. However, philosophers have rarely thematized the notion of reception or addressed questions of what one does when one studies the reception of a philosophical author, work or idea. Instead, philosophers have considered the field of reception history to be part of intellectual history, written by historians, and not part of the history of philosophy, written by philosophers.

While philosophers tend to examine ideas in themselves and as part of a particular philosophical corpus or a critical conversation between philosophers, the study of reception offers interesting and creative ways to broaden the scope of what it means to analyse and contextualize ideas. It does so by helping uncover complex processes of appropriation and development of ideas according to the changing agendas and goals of new authors and readers. Furthermore, a focus on how reception transforms philosophical ideas helps to reveal the importance of little-known figures who have previously been seen as unoriginal for having merely adopted the ideas of their predecessors, or worse still, dismissed as plagiarists. This is the case, for example, with some Early Modern authors who questioned male biases and argued for the dignity and rights of women. In the second half of the seventeenth century, François Poullain de la Barre used René Descartes’s distinction between the mind and the body, as well as the Cartesian method of doubt, to argue for the equality of the sexes and to call into question the dominant social customs and prejudices, concluding famously that ‘the sex has no mind’ ( 2002 : 82). Subsequently, Poullain’s early feminism was mentioned and used by writers who developed it further in new, even more radical directions and contexts, from the anonymous Sophia and the novelist, philosopher and biographer Mary Hays in eighteenth-century Britain, to Simone de Beauvoir in twentieth-century France (see e.g. Broad 2022 ; Reuter 2024 ; Carmel and Walker 2025 ). Exploring what these authors _did_ with the ideas that were at their disposal is at the very heart of the study of reception.

In what follows, we discuss recent scholarship on the reception of the thought of Mary Wollstonecraft (1759–1797) and analyse what forms of reception history we find in these studies. We highlight the innovations of these studies, both methodologically and philosophically, including the new understandings of Wollstonecraft’s ideas and their afterlives as they emerge from them. Our goal throughout this piece is to reflect on the possible future directions for the study of Wollstonecraft’s reception and the potential contributions of reception history to philosophy more broadly.

## 1\. Reception methods

Does the question of Wollstonecraft’s reception amount to who she influenced (and was influenced by)? Indeed, reception is often associated with the concept of influence. Quentin Skinner famously suggested three necessary conditions for demonstrating the influence of thinker A on thinker B, namely, ‘(i) that B is known to have studied A’s works; (ii) that B could not have found the relevant doctrines in any writer other than A; and (iii) that B could not have arrived at the relevant doctrines independently’ ( Skinner 2002 : 75–76). This kind of test might seem all too strict for scholars who are less interested in making strong historical claims on the relationships between thinkers. Yet it is noteworthy that such an influence of thinker A on thinker B is only _one_ possible kind of the reception of thinker A. As recent studies show, the reception of ideas can take many different shapes: from strong influences and word-to-word borrowings, through creative adaptations and apparent resemblances, to critical engagements and even disagreements (for some of these variations in the case of Thomas Hobbes’ multifaceted reception, see Carmel 2024 ; Douglass 2015 ; Goldie 1991 ; Parkin 2007 ).

Furthermore, reception often consists of taking a thinker’s ideas or methods – or integrating different ideas from several thinkers – and applying them to different cases and contexts, sometimes leading to new conclusions. The question of the reception of ideas is therefore closely related to – yet notably distinct from – the question of the original meanings or intentions behind these ideas. We can trace the ways in which a theory is used by subsequent writers – and indeed sometimes misused, if examined in relation to what it was supposed to convey in the first place – but the emphasis moves to the afterlife of the theory, or in other words, to the ways in which the theory takes on a life of its own. At the same time, the study of how an idea is received adds a significant layer to its philosophical evaluation: not necessarily asking whether it was understood correctly or not, but rather what the full philosophical potential of that idea is and what it might tell us about the way in which it is constructed. For example, the fact that Hobbes’ ideas were interpreted and employed by writers from different factions for different and sometimes even opposite goals might suggest that they were designed in an open-ended or even paradoxical manner in the first place, so as to appeal to different audiences and encourage readers to grapple with them on their own ( Parkin 2018 : 197–98). It is precisely this kind of complexity, we would like to suggest, that makes the multilayered study of reception both valuable and fascinating, and which can prove especially fruitful in the case of a groundbreaking – and sometimes also puzzling – writer such as Wollstonecraft (see also Kaplan 2002 ).

## 2\. The context of Wollstonecraft’s reception

> The intrepid spirit, daring flights, lofty pretensions, and disdain of sanctioned opinions, which characterize the productions of the vindicator of the Rights of Woman, have combined to excite an extraordinary degree of attention; which some events, of a peculiar nature, in her personal history have had a tendency to increase. … To advance on the scale of reason half the species, is no ignoble ambition. The efforts of the extraordinary woman whose life we are about to review, were directed to the emancipation of her own sex, whom she considered as sunk in a state of degradation, glorying in their weakness, voluntarily surrendering the privilege of rational agents, and contending, in her own emphatic language, 'for the sentiment that brutalized them' ( Hays 1800 : 411–12).
> 
> 

The recent interest in the reception of Wollstonecraft has in part been guided by a wish to refute the previous assumption that she was scandalized by the publication of her husband William Godwin’s _Memoirs of the Author of a Vindication of the Rights of Woman_ (1798), which openly described her unconventional love life and suicide attempts. Her works were therefore soon forgotten, the assumption continues, with her _Vindication of the Rights of Woman_ (1792) only being rediscovered by the nineteenth-century suffragist movement. Indeed, reception history can include the study of how a philosopher’s reputation was impacted by – and consequently had an impact on – the reaction to their ideas, and even how, and why, they were ‘forgotten’ and ‘recovered’ (see also Smith 2024 ; Taylor 2016 : 1–18).

The image of a scandalized-turned-forgotten Wollstonecraft was partly created by her nineteenth-century rediscovery. Elizabeth Robins Pennell, who wrote the first full-length biography of Wollstonecraft since Godwin’s _Memoirs_ , sought to defend Wollstonecraft against claims of immorality, but generated in the process a picture of Wollstonecraft’s early reception as dominated by scandal and scorn. Pennell points out that Godwin’s political radicalism, and especially his views on marriage, contributed to the scandal ( 1885 : 2–3). She correctly identifies the negative effects of Wollstonecraft’s association with Godwin. In a review of her posthumously published _The Wrongs of Woman: or, Maria_ (1798) in the _Anti-Jacobin Review and Magazine_ , Robert Bisset exclaims that ‘the principles supported and the practices recommended [by Wollstonecraft] have a very great coincidence with those inculcated by the philosopher himself [i.e., Godwin], in that part of his ‘Political Justice’ in which he describes the promiscuous intercourse of the sexes, as one of the highest improvements to result from _political justice_ ’ ( Jump 2003 : 164).

However, to grasp Wollstonecraft’s early reception in full, one must keep in mind that she was generally well-received by critics. Her previous book, _Letters Written during a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark_ (1796), for example, was widely and mostly positively reviewed ( Johnson 2020 : 46–47; Reuter forthcoming ). Not all readers sided with the _Anti-Jacobin Review and Magazine_ , even after the publication of Godwin’s _Memoirs_ and _Wrongs of Woman_ . In a recent special issue of _Women’s Writing_ , Kandice Sharren studies the holdings of Wollstonecraft’s works in commercially-run circulating libraries and membership-based subscription libraries between 1799 and 1842, that is, in the decades during which it has been assumed that Wollstonecraft was scandalized and scarcely read. Nineteen out of the thirty-six libraries surveyed held works by Wollstonecraft. _Rights of Women_ and _Letters Written during a Short Residence_ were the most frequent holdings, though several of her other works were also available in the first three decades of the nineteenth century ( Sharren 2024 : 461, 464). The sources do not show the extent to which these books were on loan, but their availability is evidence enough that Wollstonecraft’s books were not considered clandestine.

Eileen Hunt takes the refutation of claims about Wollstonecraft’s marginal influence in the first half of the nineteenth century as the explicit target of what she calls a revisionist reception history of Wollstonecraft’s thought. Following Martyn Thompson, she approaches reception history as exploring neglected questions and research strategies, suggesting that scholars ‘should be sensitive to how an author’s … readers _become_ authors in their responses to her past texts, via new editions, commentaries, and “creative misreadings”’ ( Hunt 2013 : 504; Thompson 1993 ). In her study, Hunt gives a broad overview of editions and translations of Wollstonecraft’s works published in Europe from 1792 to 1904, demonstrating that Wollstonecraft was more influential than had previously been assumed. As we will see in the final section, which is dedicated to Hunt’s recent book, this kind of overview offers great potential for Wollstonecraft’s scholarship, and at the same time raises the methodological difficulty of engaging in in-depth discussions of specific cases of reception. In this sense, Hunt’s work calls for a new research programme that invites further detailed studies of her revisionist hypothesis.

## 3\. Forms of reception

Several kinds of source texts are available to scholars interested in Wollstonecraft’s immediate reception, including (but not limited to) works by subsequent authors who explicitly or implicitly engage with Wollstonecraft’s ideas; book reviews; translations of her works, including introductions and annotations; biographical texts that often discuss her philosophical and political ideas; literary works that portray characters, either as protagonists or as caricatures, who embody the ideals of women’s emancipation associated with Wollstonecraft and her milieu (e.g. Hays 2009 ; Weiss 2017 ; but also including satires such as Richard Polwhele’s misogynistic poem _The Unsex’d Females_ of 1798, aimed at Wollstonecraft and others); and finally, diaries, correspondence and private manuscripts, which can shed a new and unique light on the reception of writers, especially within their immediate intellectual and social milieus. As Mark Philp has recently shown, this was the case with London’s ‘radical literary women’ of the 1790s, among whom one can find evidence of various interconnections in their correspondences. The writer Elizabeth Inchbald’s remark to Godwin on Wollstonecraft can serve as an example: ‘I did _not_ know her’, she insisted, ‘I never wished to know her – as I avoid every female acquaintance who has no husband, I avoided her’, thus demonstrating that Inchbald ‘was clearly unwilling to put at risk what she already had’ socially, however much she may have sympathized with Godwin and Wollstonecraft intellectually ( Philp 2020 : 133–34).

It is not too surprising that works that engage with Wollstonecraft’s ideas are still largely understudied, given that many of her followers remain even more neglected in the scholarship than she had been until only recently. Yet recent scholarship has demonstrated how reviews and translations of her works in particular offered spaces for thorough philosophical engagements with her thought. In what follows, we discuss this scholarship, ending with Eileen Hunt’s _Portraits of Wollstonecraft_ (2021), which broadens the scope of reception materials even further.

### 3\.1 Reviews

Some of the most explicit early discussions of Wollstonecraft’s ideas are found in the reviews of her books published throughout Europe. Harriet Jump has collected the critical reception in British periodicals in an edited work that reprints nineteen reviews published in the 1790s. In her introduction, Jump presents a concise overview of the responses that each of Wollstonecraft’s reviewed works received while placing the periodicals in their contexts ( Jump 2003 : 2–4). In the recent edited volume _Mary Wollstonecraft in Context_ , Nancy E. Johnson and Alessa Johns examine British and German reviews of Wollstonecraft’s works respectively, demonstrating that Wollstonecraft was indeed recognized and that her writings were perceived as both political and philosophical texts ( Johnson 2020 : 41–49; Johns 2020 : 323–31, especially 327–28).

Recent scholarship has thus paid increasing attention to Wollstonecraft’s international reception. Isabelle Bour, for example, compares British and French reviews of Wollstonecraft’s _Rights of Woman_ and _Wrongs of Woman_ while paying a close attention to the topics discussed in the reviews. She argues that although British reviews of _Rights of Woman_ varied from laudatory to profoundly negative, the French reviews were altogether ‘more aware of the theoretical import of her work’ ( 2013 : 575). Even a short review in _Chronique de Paris_ summarizes the core of Wollstonecraft’s defence of women, including her criticism of male tyranny. An extensive review in _Journal encyclopédique_ sides with Wollstonecraft (and clearly, though implicitly, against Jean-Jacques Rousseau) on the topic of whether there are differences between the virtues of women and men, arguing that there are no sexual virtues and that all duties are shared by all human beings (577–79).

The contrast between the British and French receptions becomes especially stark when comparing reviews of _Wrongs of Woman_ . Whereas the three British reviews Bour discusses focus on the low moral qualities of the book, the one French review she has found, published in _Journal de Paris_ , possibly by Pierre-Louis Roederer, discusses it within ‘the genre of the novel as discourse on manners and passions, a kind of discourse which … has a cognitive function’ and is related to the sciences of history and nature (583). Bour emphasizes that in Roederer’s reading, _Wrongs of Woman_ becomes a novel that contributes to the knowledge of human passions and a _roman à thèse_ – a political novel – which articulates a detailed analysis of the subordination of women. As she explains, Roederer describes _Wrongs of Woman_ as a philosophical and sentimental novel: philosophical in the sense of the novel’s criticism of injustice; sentimental with respect to ‘the facts which make up its fabric, through the flow and the colouring of its style’ (584, 587). Roederer’s engagement with _Wrongs of Woman_ is significantly different, for instance, from that of Robert Bisset, who in the _Anti-Jacobin Review and Magazine_ interprets Wollstonecraft’s criticism of marriage as promiscuity grounded in Godwin’s conception of political justice, not even allowing Wollstonecraft to spell out her own moral and political principles ( Jump 2003 : 164).

### 3\.2 Translations

Translations of philosophical texts are in themselves philosophical interpretations which engage with ideas originally developed in another language. Introductions and footnotes to translations also serve various functions, including detailed philosophical dialogues with the author of the original text. Scholarship on translations of Wollstonecraft’s works has mainly focused on the French and German translations of _Rights of Woman_ and the French translation of _Wrongs of Woman_ . Isabelle Bour and Laura Kirkley in particular have demonstrated how differences in grammar and lexicon contribute to the production of various meaning in these texts. For example, Bour traces the language of sensibility as it evolves in the French translations of _Rights of Woman_ and _Wrongs of Woman_ . She shows that though expressions of sensibility were essential to novels throughout Europe in the second half of the eighteenth century, their linguistic signifiers were quite different in English and French. Bour argues that Basile-Joseph Ducos, who translated _Wrongs of Woman_ , was especially aware of the complexities of the lexicon of sensibility and the philosophical implications of its translation. His translation of philosophical meanings, rather than individual words, is particularly visible in the case of the English _sympathy_ , which is not translated into the etymologically close French _sympathie_ , but _mouvement de compassion_ or _pitié_ ( Bour 2004 : 501). In addition to reflecting Wollstonecraft’s understanding of sympathy, Ducos’ interpretation seems to indicate familiarity with Scottish discussions of sympathy, introduced in France by Sophie de Grouchy’s French translation of Adam Smith’s _Theory of Moral Sentiments_ and her own critical engagement with his theory in her _Lettres sur la sympathie_ (1798). Here, we find an interesting example of intertwined receptions, where the engagement with one philosopher shapes the engagement with another (see also Bergès 2024 : 450). Kirkley, too, highlights the significance of Ducos’ translational choices and contexts, but her take on his work is more critical than Bour’s. Thus, she states, Ducos’ ‘interpretation of the sentimental conventions deployed and undercut in Wollstonecraft’s text reveals that certain ideological and poetological factors impinging on his translational strategy can also combine to suppress the most ambitious aspects of Wollstonecraft’s feminism’, for example, where he reaches ‘conventional interpretations of certain key passages, such that his translation obscures the most innovative aspects of Wollstonecraft’s analysis of erotic love’ ( 2015 : 244–45).

More recently, Bour and Kirkley have revisited the French translation of Wollstonecraft’s _Rights of Woman_ . Bour (2022) presents a well-substantiated hypothesis about the identity of the anonymous translator–annotator of the book. She revises her earlier assumption of a male translator and argues that the book was translated by Félicité Brissot de Warville, who authored some footnotes, whereas most footnotes were by her husband Jacques-Pierre Brissot de Warville. Kirkley then shows how the translation helps ‘to fuse Wollstonecraft’s feminist arguments with republican propaganda’, whereas some footnotes criticize her use of theological arguments and her supposedly insufficient anticlericalism ( 2022 : 7–8). Additionally, Sandrine Bergès suggests that Wollstonecraft’s influence on French revolutionary educational reform was ‘both historical and philosophical’, in the sense that ‘her ideas were sought at a later stage, and the eventual legislation regarding women’s education is based on an argument that she made the most strongly and plausibly, that mothers must be educated’ ( 2024 : 452). Taken together, these interpretations of Wollstonecraft’s early French reception constitute a detailed study that proves the value of critical scholarly dialogue.

Wollstonecraft’s fame in France lived on well into the nineteenth century. In 1826, the eclectic author César Gardeton published a book titled _Les droits des femmes, et l’injustice des hommes; par Mistriss Godwin_ , which is a reprint of the French translation (1750–1751) of the English work _Woman Not Inferior to Man_ (1739) by the pseudonymous Sophia. In 1831, the Brazilian feminist Nísia Floresta adapted Gardeton’s edition into Portuguese, believing she was engaging with Wollstonecraft. Therefore, Floresta contributed to a Latin American reception of Wollstonecraft where some known facts about her life and thought merged with Sophia’s treatise ( Hunt and Matthews 2014 : 70, 78–80). Interestingly, as noted above, _Woman Not Inferior to Man_ was inspired by the 1673 treatise _On the Equality of the Two Sexes_ by Poullain de la Barre. This case thus shows the transformation and integration of ideas from several sources, while travelling from seventeenth-century France, via Britain, to nineteenth-century Latin America, and forming the core of early feminist thought. There is further research to be done on exactly what happened during this journey, which also demonstrates that reception research can benefit immensely from taking a global perspective.

Though the French reception of _Rights of Woman_ and _Wrongs of Woman_ has so far received the most scholarly attention, and was, as Johns puts it, ‘vigorous and furthered by elevated social figures’, the German reception was ‘even more copious and prolonged’ ( Johns 2020 : 325). One notable such case was _Rettung der rechte des weibes_ (1793–1794), the German translation of _Rights of Woman_ , prepared by the pedagogues C.G. Salzmann and G.F.C. Weissenborn. As Kirkley suggests, this case further demonstrates ‘the role translation plays in transmitting different versions of Wollstonecraft’s texts to the diverse readerships of Western Europe and creating a complex translational afterlife for her feminism’ ( 2022 : 1). Even when looking at the reception of one book in one context, we can still find distinguishable interpretations with different points of emphasis: while Weissenborn’s translation underlines the criticism of male subordination of women, Salzmann’s introduction and footnotes seem to soften the tone of the twofold critique of male and political tyranny ( Kirkley 2022 : 15–17; see also Kirkley 2009 : 167–68). Moreover, Johns adds, Weissenborn’s full engagement with Wollstonecraft’s thought reveals that he ‘furthered her ideas’ in other writings to argue ‘that women, like men, need the human right to find happiness in the pursuit of a calling they themselves select’ ( 2020 : 326).

The German translation of _Rights of Woman_ was influential beyond the German language through chain translations into Dutch in 1796 and Danish in 1801 ( Johns 2020 : 328). The German and Danish cultural spheres were close, not least in the field of education, and indeed the Danish translation is entirely based on the German, including Salzmann’s introduction and footnotes. As Anne-Birgitte Rønning has shown, the Danish translation was published as a gift book for female readers, thus exemplifying a transfer of ideas within a patriarchal structure ( 2019 : 294, 306). In this process, Wollstonecraft’s political radicalism might have become diluted, but at the same time, thanks to a new format, it reached a readership that would probably not have been able to access it otherwise.

### 3\.3 Hunt’s portraits and the future of Wollstonecraft’s reception

Eileen Hunt has recently expanded her scholarship on the reception of Wollstonecraft by editing _Portraits of Wollstonecraft_ (2021), published in two volumes. Here, she revisits her abovementioned revisionist reception history hypothesis by collecting source materials to substantiate a new understanding of how Wollstonecraft has been perceived from her day to our own. Unlike Jump, who included contemporary scholarship on Wollstonecraft in her edited volumes, Hunt has limited recent materials to artistic and other non-academic representations of Wollstonecraft. Considering the expansion of Wollstonecraft scholarship in recent decades, this is well-justified (see e.g. Reuter 2022 ; Taylor 2003 ; Tomaselli 2021 ). Also, unlike Jump, Hunt aims for a global scope including, for example, translations of an essay published in Brazil in 1885 on Wollstonecraft as an educator, the commentary in the first Japanese translation of _Rights of Woman_ from 1980 and the preface to the first Chinese edition from 1992 ( Hunt 2021 : 237–47, 617–20, 623–25).

One of the great merits of Hunt’s volumes is therefore that they collect and make available a wide range of key texts, translated into English. Another important merit is Hunt’s systematic effort to collect visual representations of Wollstonecraft, covering artworks up to 2020, when Maggi Hambling’s much-debated statue commemorating Wollstonecraft was unveiled (653–58). Many of the artworks studied are contextual, such as a British suffrage banner by the stained-glass artist Mary Lowndes bearing the text ‘Mary Wollstonecraft Pioneer’ (542–43). The mere tracing of images is a herculean effort. By doing so, Hunt adds a widely neglected and highly innovative layer of reception, namely, visual and artistic representations, showing that reception is not limited to intellectual or even textual sources in the traditional sense, and reminding us of the extent to which different kinds of representations constitute an intrinsic and valuable part of the lives and afterlives of thinkers and ideas.

Hunt’s work demonstrates that both written and visual portrayals are indeed essential aspects of reception, and her discussion of early portraits of Wollstonecraft is particularly helpful and well-researched. Moreover, when studying historical sources discussing Wollstonecraft and her thought, it is useful to have Hunt’s collection of visual images at hand, not least because texts may refer to images without having the means to reproduce them. For instance, an obituary of Wollstonecraft published in Swedish in the newspaper _Åbo Tidningar_ in 1798, translated mostly from a German obituary, remarked: ‘The translator, who owns her portrait, may add that in her face a feminine charm is joined to a masculine thought’ (quoted in Reuter forthcoming ). Together with some additional evidence, Hunt’s book makes it easy to establish that the portrait in question is most likely William Ridley’s engraving from 1796 ( 2021 : 30–32; see also Kirkley 2009 : 159).

Hunt’s aim has been to include texts that each ‘[mark] an important date for Wollstonecraft’s reception into a particular culture, language, or literary form’ ( 2021 : xvii). This is indeed a welcome goal. Yet in cases where there is little research to date, such as in the Danish and Swedish contexts, the picture that arises may be somewhat partial. For example, the volumes include a preface to the first Swedish translation of _Wrong of Woman_ from 1799, which conveys a rather apologetic representation of Wollstonecraft, perhaps since it is dedicated to the translator’s aunt, who apparently had a different outlook on life: ‘I know it’, writes the preface’s author, ‘you do not like, that one complains about injustices endured: the oppressed Virtue ought to forgive and forget. But you shall not find this Book completely without value’ (200–201). Such a portrayal differs, for instance, from even earlier Swedish representations of Wollstonecraft as a perceptive enlightened philosopher, found in other sources not included by Hunt ( Reuter 2023 , forthcoming ).

To be sure, the choice of one source text over another – an inevitable part of any editorial undertaking, especially of the breadth of Hunt’s work – does not undermine the great value of these volumes. Rather, it demonstrates the extent to which reception history is teamwork, an enterprise that requires interdisciplinarity as well as detailed knowledge of a wide variety of linguistic, cultural and political contexts. Perhaps most importantly, these volumes provide us with an impressively vast range of materials, which can now be studied further by scholars. In this sense, Hunt succeeds to achieve precisely what she had set out to do already a decade ago, namely, to ‘inspire other scholars to study in greater detail the reception of Wollstonecraft in Europe and elsewhere’ (2013: 527). As she has also observed: ‘The international and transnational character of feminism is a vital historical, political, and philosophical topic. Tracing Wollstonecraft’s international reception in non-European, and particularly non-Western and post-colonial cultures and nations, will be an important next step in understanding the richness of the intellectual history of feminism’ (527).

Hunt’s recent volumes will most certainly inspire scholars of numerous disciplines – philosophy as well as history, politics, literature and gender studies, to name just a few – to come up with new avenues of research into the multiple legacies of a truly revolutionary and multifaceted philosopher, whose ideas have as much to offer today as they did when they were first written, read, translated and reviewed.
